title: Dog Ate Xylitol Gum: Urgent Help & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate gum with xylitol, act fast. Xylitol is highly toxic to dogs. Follow this guide for emergency steps and veterinary triage info.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol ingestion is a life-threatening veterinary emergency for dogs that requires immediate professional care to prevent fatal blood sugar drops and liver failure.

What is xylitol and why is it dangerous for dogs?

Xylitol is a common sugar substitute found in many sugar-free products, including chewing gum, breath mints, and some peanut butters. While harmless to humans, it is a potent toxin for dogs because it triggers a rapid, massive release of insulin from their pancreas.

Is xylitol ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Yes, this is a life-threatening emergency with a high urgency level.
  • Ingestion can cause a sudden, dangerous drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia).
  • Exposure can lead to acute liver failure or even death if not treated immediately by a professional.

How does a photo of the product packaging help with my dog's triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the packaging, specifically the ingredient list and the total weight of the product.
  • Different brands contain varying concentrations of xylitol, making the label vital for safety calculations.
  • A photo allows your veterinarian to calculate the exact toxic dose consumed and prioritize life-saving treatment accordingly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol ingestion stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release, potentially causing profound hypoglycemia at dosages >75-100 mg/kg. Ingestion >500 mg/kg may lead to severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Hypoglycemia signs (vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, coma) can appear within 30 minutes or be delayed up to 12-18 hours. Liver injury signs may be delayed up to 24-48 hours post-ingestion, though liver enzyme increases can be detected within 8-12 hours. Emesis should only be attempted under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals. Activated charcoal is not recommended as it does not bind xylitol. If >75-100 mg/kg has been ingested, hospitalize and monitor blood glucose every 1-2 hours for 12 hours; if >500 mg/kg, also monitor liver values every 24 hours for 72 hours. Treat hypoglycemia with IV dextrose boluses or constant-rate infusions until the dog maintains normal blood glucose without supplementation. For ingestions >500 mg/kg, administer dextrose even if normoglycemic, and consider hepatoprotectants such as N-acetylcysteine, S-adenosylmethionine, and silymarin.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How quickly do symptoms of xylitol poisoning appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms such as vomiting, loss of coordination, or lethargy can appear as quickly as 15 to 30 minutes after ingestion.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog acts sick?
faqs.answer: No. Waiting for symptoms is dangerous because liver damage or severe hypoglycemia can occur before you notice outward signs.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not attempt to induce vomiting unless explicitly directed by a veterinarian, as this can be dangerous if your dog is already becoming hypoglycemic.
